pattern: Reflecting the list around its middle turns the front-node/back-node twin relationship into two pointers walking together from the center outward.
hints: A twin sum only ever pairs the i-th node from the front with the i-th node from the back, so you never need to look at more than half the list at once.
Find the middle with a fast/slow pointer, then reverse the second half in place so it now reads from the center outward instead of from the tail inward.
Walk the untouched first half and the reversed second half together one step at a time, summing corresponding nodes, and keep a running maximum.

edge_inputs.description: small even-length list, outer pair is the maximum twin sum
small even-length list, an inner pair beats the outer pair
minimum possible length, two nodes at the max node value
minimum length, two nodes at the min node value
outer values are small but a middle pair is far larger, catches solutions that assume the outer pair always wins
length 8 palindrome-shaped values to exercise the reversal walk beyond a single pair
all nodes share one value, every twin sum is identical
strictly increasing values so the true maximum twin sum is the very first/last pair, not any middle pair
longer list mixing low and high values near the extremes

stress.description: random even-length lists with random node values across the full allowed range
stress.note: size is clamped to an even number so the list always has a well-defined twin for every node

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def pairSum(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> int:
        slow, fast = head, head
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        prev = None
        curr = slow
        while curr:
            nxt = curr.next
            curr.next = prev
            prev = curr
            curr = nxt
        max_sum = 0
        first = head
        second = prev
        while second:
            max_sum = max(max_sum, first.val + second.val)
            first = first.next
            second = second.next
        return max_sum
